title: Disorders of Smell and Taste
authors: Richard L. Doty, Steven M. Bromley

items.title: Olfactory Dysfunction as a Predictor of Mortality in the Elderly
items.content: A longitudinal study demonstrated that elderly individuals with lower baseline olfactory test scores have significantly higher mortality rates over four years compared to those with higher scores.
keyPoints: Lowest baseline olfactory test scores correlate with a 45% mortality rate over 4 years.
Highest baseline olfactory test scores correlate with an 18% mortality rate over the same period.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.title: Olfactory Dysfunction as an Early Indicator of Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Decreased smell function can be one of the first signs of neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease (PD) and Alzheimer's disease (AD), indicating their presymptomatic phase.
keyPoints: Olfactory dysfunction is an early sign in PD and AD.
Signifies the 'presymptomatic' phase of these neurodegenerative diseases.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.title: Olfactory System: Unique Receptor Diversity and Regeneration
items.content: The olfactory system is characterized by a vast array of nearly 400 types of G-protein-coupled odor receptors, each expressed on individual receptor cells. This diversity is unmatched in other sensory systems. Additionally, the olfactory receptor cells have regenerative capabilities through stem cell replacement near the basement membrane.
keyPoints: Nearly 400 types of GPCRs are expressed on olfactory receptor cells.
Each olfactory receptor cell expresses only one type of GPCR.
Receptor cells can be replaced by stem cells, although this process is often incomplete.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Olfactory Pathway: Synaptic Organization and Modulation
items.content: The olfactory pathway involves receptor cell axons passing through the cribriform plate to synapse in the glomeruli of the olfactory bulbs. Each glomerulus acts as a functional unit, receiving inputs from receptor cells expressing the same type of GPCR. The activity of mitral and tufted cells is modulated by periglomerular cells, secondary dendrites, and granule cells within the bulb.
keyPoints: Receptor cell axons synapse in glomeruli within olfactory bulbs.
Each glomerulus receives inputs from receptor cells expressing the same GPCR type.
Mitral/tufted cell activity is modulated by periglomerular, secondary dendrites, and granule cells.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Taste System: Receptor Diversity and Mechanisms
items.content: The taste system utilizes specialized receptor cells within taste buds to detect tastants. Sweet and umami tastes are mediated by T1R receptors, while bitter tastes rely on approximately 30 types of T2R GPCRs. Sour sensations are detected via the PKD2L1 receptor, and salty tastes result from Na⁺ ion entry through amiloride-sensitive channels.
keyPoints: Sweet and umami tastes mediated by T1R1, T1R2, and T1R3 receptors.
~30 types of T2R GPCRs mediate bitter taste sensations.
Sour tastants sensed by PKD2L1 receptor (TRP family).
Salty taste arises from Na⁺ ion entry via amiloride-sensitive channels.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Taste Receptors Beyond the Oral Cavity
items.content: Taste receptors, including bitter and sweet types, are present in various body parts such as the alimentary and respiratory tracts. These receptors play roles in metabolism and protection against pathogens.
keyPoints: Brush cells expressing $ \alpha $-gustducin found in human trachea, lung, pancreas, and gallbladder.
T1R3 receptors and gustducin are crucial for dietary sugar sensing and hormone regulation in the gut.
T2R38 receptor responds to Pseudomonas aeruginosa molecules, influencing susceptibility to respiratory infections.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Pathways for Taste Information Transmission
items.content: Taste information is transmitted to the brain via cranial nerves VII, IX, and X. Additionally, CN V contributes somatosensory inputs that enhance taste perception.
keyPoints: CN VII innervates the anterior tongue and soft palate; CN IX innervates the posterior tongue; CN X innervates laryngeal surfaces.
CN V (V3) conveys touch, burning, cooling, irritation to the brain, enhancing taste experience.
Chorda tympani nerve carries parasympathetic fibers influencing saliva production.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Neural Pathways for Taste Processing
items.content: Taste information is processed in the brainstem, thalamus, and cortical areas, forming a pathway from taste buds to conscious recognition.
keyPoints: Axons synapse with taste buds enter the nucleus of the solitary tract (NTS) in the medulla.
Neurons project to the ventroposteromedial thalamic nucleus (VPM) via the medial lemniscus.
Projections from VPM reach the primary taste cortex (PTC) and secondary taste cortex (caudolateral OFC), involved in flavor recognition.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Olfactory Dysfunction
items.content: Olfactory dysfunction is prevalent in the general population, with significant decrements observed especially in older adults. This condition can lead to nutritional disturbances and increased risk of accidental gas poisonings.
keyPoints: Overall prevalence of olfactory dysfunction: 13.5% (NHANES 2013–2014)
>50% of the population aged 65-80 years have significant smell decrements
75% of those aged ≥80 years experience significant smell loss
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.title: Common Causes and Mechanisms of Olfactory Loss
items.content: The most common identifiable causes of long-lasting or permanent olfactory dysfunction are severe upper respiratory infections, head trauma, and chronic rhinosinusitis. The mechanisms include damage to the olfactory filaments and epithelium.
keyPoints: Top three causes: severe upper respiratory infections, head trauma, chronic rhinosinusitis
<10% recovery of age-related normal function in posttraumatic anosmia over time
25% recovery in those with less-than-total loss
Smell loss from COVID-19 can persist in 5–10% of cases
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.title: Olfactory Dysfunction in Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Several neurodegenerative diseases are associated with olfactory impairment, often preceding clinical diagnosis. This includes Parkinson's disease and dementia with Lewy bodies.
keyPoints: Neurodegenerative diseases with olfactory impairment: PD, AD, Huntington's disease, DLB, etc.
Olfactory dysfunction in PD can precede clinical diagnosis by years
Smell loss is more pronounced in early DLB than mild AD
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.title: Orexin Deficiency Linked to Olfactory Impairment in Narcolepsy
items.content: Narcoleptic patients often experience olfactory impairment due to loss of hypothalamic neurons expressing orexin neuropeptides. Orexin-containing neurons project throughout the olfactory system, and damage to these projections may underlie impaired olfaction in narcolepsy. Intranasal administration of orexin A has been shown to improve olfactory function.
keyPoints: Orexin deficiency is directly responsible for olfactory loss in narcolepsy with cataplexy.
Intranasal orexin A improves olfactory function in narcoleptic patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.title: Taste Dysfunction Often Due to Olfactory Loss
items.content: Most taste dysfunction cases are actually due to olfactory loss, as many flavors depend on retronasal olfactory stimulation. Taste buds mediate basic tastes and regenerate, making significant gustatory impairment rare except in systemic conditions or medication use.
keyPoints: Taste dysfunction is often secondary to olfactory loss.
Significant whole-mouth gustatory function impairment is rare outside of generalized metabolic disturbances.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.title: Medications Commonly Cause Taste Alterations
items.content: Over 250 medications can alter taste, with antineoplastic agents, antirheumatic drugs, antibiotics, and blood pressure medications being major offenders. Terbinafine may cause prolonged taste disturbance, while eszopiclone is associated with bitter dysgeusia, more common in women.
keyPoints: >250 medications can alter taste ability.
Antineoplastic agents, antirheumatic drugs, antibiotics, and blood pressure medications are major offenders.
Eszopiclone causes bitter dysgeusia, stronger in women.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.title: Identifying Etiology of Chemosensory Problems through Clinical History
items.content: A detailed clinical history can help establish the probable cause of chemosensory issues. Sudden loss may indicate head trauma, ischemia, infection, or psychiatric conditions, while gradual loss might suggest progressive lesions or post-traumatic changes. Intermittent loss often points to inflammation.
keyPoints: Sudden loss suggests head trauma, ischemia, infection, or psychiatric condition.
Gradual loss can indicate a progressive obstructive lesion or follow head trauma.
Intermittent loss suggests an inflammatory process.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.title: Importance of Medication and Comorbid Conditions in Chemosensory Disturbances
items.content: Assessing medications and comorbid conditions is crucial, as many drugs can cause chemosensory disturbances. Conditions like renal failure, liver disease, hypothyroidism, diabetes, or dementia are associated with smell impairment.
keyPoints: Many medications can cause chemosensory disturbances.
Comorbid conditions such as renal failure, liver disease, hypothyroidism, diabetes, and dementia should be assessed.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.title: Utilizing Quantitative Sensory Testing for Objective Assessment
items.content: Quantitative sensory testing is recommended to objectively assess chemosensory function. Self-reports can be misleading, and standardized tests like the 40-item University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test (UPSIT) provide reliable data.
keyPoints: Self-reports may not accurately reflect chemosensory dysfunction.
The UPSIT is a widely used olfactory test with norms based on over 10,000 subjects.
Quantitative testing helps in legal claims and assessing treatment effects.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.title: Condition-Specific Management for Olfactory/Gustatory Disturbances
items.content: Management of olfactory and gustatory disturbances is tailored to the underlying condition. Specific treatments are beneficial for conditions like hypothyroidism, diabetes, or infections affecting chemoreception.
keyPoints: Specific treatments correct underlying diseases influencing chemoreception in hypothyroidism, diabetes, or infections.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Role of Glucocorticoids in Olfactory Dysfunction
items.content: Intranasal and systemic glucocorticoids can be effective for olfactory dysfunction due to inflammatory conditions. Oral prednisone is commonly used, while intranasal administration requires specific techniques like the Moffett's position.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids help in reducing local edema and scar tissue deposition post head trauma.
Oral prednisone is a common approach; intranasal glucocorticoids are more effective with specific administration techniques.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Recovery Predictors and Interventions for Chemosensory Loss
items.content: Spontaneous recovery can occur in chemosensory loss, with prognosis best predicted by initial dysfunction severity. Repeated odor exposure may aid recovery, though its efficacy over spontaneous improvement is uncertain.
keyPoints: Prognosis of smell loss depends on initial dysfunction severity, age, and duration before testing.
11% of anosmic and 23% of hyposmic patients regain normal function over about 4 years.
Repeated smelling of odors may aid recovery; $\alpha$-lipoic acid (400 mg/d) might help post-viral infection smell loss.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Vitamin D Supplementation During Chemotherapy
items.content: Adding vitamin D3, specifically at a dosage of 1000–2000 units per day, may benefit patients experiencing smell and taste complaints during or following chemotherapy. This is due to its role in mitigating chemotherapy-induced mucocutaneous toxicity and dysgeusia.
keyPoints: Vitamin D deficiency can exacerbate chemotherapy-induced mucocutaneous toxicity and dysgeusia.
Recommended dosage: 1000–2000 units of vitamin D3 per day.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.title: Alternative Therapies for Chemosensory Disturbance
items.content: Non-pharmacological approaches such as acupuncture, meditation, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and yoga can assist patients in managing the psychosocial stressors associated with chemosensory disturbances. Additionally, modifying diet to enhance other sensory experiences of a meal can improve overall eating satisfaction.
keyPoints: Alternative therapies include acupuncture, meditation, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and yoga.
Diet modification by enhancing food texture, aroma, temperature, and color can optimize the eating experience.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a significant consequence of dysfunctional olfactory senses?
items.explanation: Dysfunctional olfactory senses can lead to untoward physical and psychological consequences.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.question: What was the mortality rate over a 4-year period for elderly persons with the lowest baseline olfactory test scores?
items.explanation: The study found that those with the lowest baseline olfactory test scores had a 45% mortality rate over a 4-year period.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.question: Which neurodegenerative diseases are associated with decreased smell function as an early sign?
options: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Huntington's disease
Multiple Sclerosis and Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
items.explanation: Decreased smell function is an early sign of Parkinson's disease (PD) and Alzheimer's disease (AD).
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.question: Where are olfactory receptor cell axons located within the nasal cavity?
options: On the cilia of olfactory receptor cells
Within a unique neuroepithelium covering the cribriform plate, superior nasal septum, and superior turbinate
In the glomeruli of the olfactory bulbs
Embedded in the fungiform papillae on the tongue
items.explanation: The cilia, dendrites, cell bodies, and proximal axonal segments of olfactory receptor cells are located within a unique neuroepithelium covering the cribriform plate, superior nasal septum, and superior turbinate.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which type of receptors mediate sweet and umami taste sensations?
options: A small family of three GPCRs, namely T1R1, T1R2, and T1R3
items.explanation: Sweet and umami taste sensations are mediated by a small family of three GPCRs: T1R1, T1R2, and T1R3.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of granule cells in the olfactory bulb?
options: They synapse with receptor cell axons within glomeruli
They are largely GABAergic and modulate the output of mitral/tufted cells
They project to the primary olfactory cortex
They express T2R receptors for bitter taste
items.explanation: Granule cells in the olfactory bulb are largely GABAergic and modulate the output of mitral/tufted cells.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: How do sour tastants activate taste receptor cells?
options: Through the entry of Na⁺ ions via specialized membrane channels
Via a small family of three GPCRs
Using the PKD2L1 receptor, a member of the TRP family
items.explanation: Sour tastants are sensed by the PKD2L1 receptor, which is part of the transient receptor potential protein (TRP) family.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which cranial nerve innervates the anterior tongue and all of the soft palate?
items.explanation: CN VII, the facial nerve, is responsible for innervating the anterior tongue and all of the soft palate.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which receptor family is involved in airway protection by increasing beat frequency of motile cilia in response to bitter compounds?
items.explanation: The T2R family of bitter receptors is involved in airway protection by increasing the beat frequency of motile cilia.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Where do taste information axons synapse before projecting to higher brain centers?
items.explanation: The axons of projection cells that carry taste information synapse in the nucleus of the solitary tract (NTS) within the medulla.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which receptor is expressed in human upper respiratory epithelia and responds to acyl-monosialactone molecules secreted by Pseudomonas aeruginosa?
items.explanation: The T2R38 taste receptor is expressed in human upper respiratory epithelia and responds to acyl-monosialactone molecules.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which factor is NOT typically associated with a higher prevalence of olfactory dysfunction?
items.explanation: While age, gender, and general health are factors influencing olfactory function, nutritional disturbances are a consequence rather than a cause of smell loss.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.question: What is the most common identifiable cause of long-lasting or permanent smell loss?
items.explanation: The passage states that severe upper respiratory infections, head trauma, and chronic rhinosinusitis are the most common causes of long-lasting or permanent smell loss, with head trauma being the most frequent.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.question: Which neurodegenerative disease is associated with minimal or nonexistent olfactory impairment?
items.explanation: The passage notes that smell loss is minimal or nonexistent in progressive supranuclear palsy.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of posttraumatic anosmic patients typically recover age-related normal olfactory function over time?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that less than 10% of posttraumatic anosmic patients will recover age-related normal function over time.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.question: Which neuropeptide deficiency is associated with impaired olfactory function in narcoleptic patients?
items.explanation: Loss of hypothalamic neurons expressing orexin (also known as hypocretin) neuropeptides is believed to be responsible for impaired olfactory function in narcoleptic patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.question: What treatment has been shown to improve olfactory function in narcoleptic patients?
items.explanation: Administration of intranasal orexin A (hypocretin-1) improved olfactory function in narcoleptic patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.question: Which cranial nerve is relatively protected along its path, reducing the likelihood of injury?
items.explanation: CN IX is relatively protected along its path, although iatrogenic interventions can result in selective injury.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.question: Which condition has been associated with a gustatory prodrome or aura?
items.explanation: On rare occasions, migraine is associated with a gustatory prodrome or aura.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.question: Which of the following conditions might be suggested by a sudden loss of smell?
options: Head trauma, ischemia, infection, or psychiatric condition
Gradual development of an obstructive lesion
items.explanation: Sudden loss suggests head trauma, ischemia, infection, or a psychiatric condition.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.question: What might intermittent loss of smell suggest?
items.explanation: Intermittent loss suggests an inflammatory process.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically assessed in patients with smell impairment?
items.explanation: Comorbid conditions like renal failure, liver disease, and hypothyroidism are assessed; cardiac arrhythmia is not.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.question: What syndrome involves raised intracranial pressure plus a compressive optic neuropathy?
items.explanation: Foster Kennedy syndrome involves raised intracranial pressure and a compressive optic neuropathy.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.question: Which treatment is specifically mentioned as potentially beneficial for taste problems secondary to candidiasis?
items.explanation: Antifungal and antibiotic treatments may reverse taste problems secondary to candidiasis.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is a common approach for using glucocorticoids in intranasal or sinus-related inflammatory conditions?
options: Systemic administration with tapering course of oral prednisone
items.explanation: One common approach is to use a tapering course of oral prednisone.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: What position enhances the effectiveness of intranasal glucocorticoids?
items.explanation: Intranasal glucocorticoids are more effective if administered in the Moffett's position.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is a predictor of prognosis for patients with smell loss?
items.explanation: The amount of dysfunction at the time of presentation was the best predictor of prognosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which vitamin derivative is known to play an essential role in the survival of olfactory neurons?
items.explanation: Retinoids, which are bioactive derivatives of vitamin A, are known to play an essential role in the survival of olfactory neurons.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential benefit of adding Vitamin D3 during or following chemotherapy?
options: Improves taste function secondary to hepatic deficiencies
items.explanation: Vitamin D3 may benefit some patients with smell and taste complaints during or following chemotherapy by reducing mucocutaneous toxicity and dysgeusia.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which alternative therapy is NOT mentioned as a way to manage chemosensory disturbance?
items.explanation: The passage mentions acupuncture, meditation, and cognitive-behavioral therapy as alternative therapies for managing chemosensory disturbance but does not mention physical therapy.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.statement: A basic understanding of olfaction and gustation is not critical for physicians.
items.explanation: Physicians need a basic understanding of these senses because many patients present with chemosensory dysfunction.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.statement: Decreased smell function can be an early indicator of neurodegenerative diseases.
items.explanation: Decreased smell function is among the first signs of neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.statement: Olfactory receptor cells can be replaced by stem cells near the basement membrane.
items.explanation: Receptor cells in the olfactory system can indeed be replaced by stem cells located near the basement membrane.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: The initial afferent projections of olfaction bypass the thalamus.
items.explanation: Olfaction is unique in that its initial afferent projections directly reach the primary olfactory cortex, bypassing the thalamus.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Taste information is only transmitted via the facial nerve.
items.explanation: Taste information is transmitted via three cranial nerves: CN VII, CN IX, and CN X.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: The chorda tympani nerve carries parasympathetic fibers to the submandibular and sublingual glands.
items.explanation: The chorda tympani nerve does carry parasympathetic fibers to these glands.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Olfactory dysfunction is more prevalent in men compared to women.
items.explanation: The passage states that women typically outperform men on tests of olfactory function.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.statement: Smell loss in COVID-19 is related to nasal inflammation.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that smell loss associated with COVID-19 is independent of nasal inflammation.

items.statement: Taste buds mediate all flavors attributed to taste.
items.explanation: Most flavors attributed to taste depend on retronasal stimulation of the olfactory receptors; taste buds only mediate basic tastes.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.statement: Dysgeusia is a common symptom in burning mouth syndrome.
items.explanation: Burning mouth syndrome, also termed glossodynia or glossalgia, can include dysgeusia as one of its symptoms.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.statement: Quantitative smell and taste testing is unnecessary for assessing chemosensory dysfunction.
items.explanation: Quantitative sensory testing provides objective information, as self-reports can be misleading.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.statement: The University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test (UPSIT) uses norms based on over 10,000 normal subjects.
items.explanation: The UPSIT is widely used and employs norms from a large sample size.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.statement: Spontaneous recovery can occur in patients with chemosensory loss or primary injury to neural pathways.
items.explanation: The passage states that spontaneous recovery can occur despite limited treatments.

items.statement: Patients with hyposmia are guaranteed improvement through repeated smelling of odors.
items.explanation: It remains to be determined how much improvement occurs over spontaneous recovery.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.statement: Zinc supplementation is universally effective in treating olfactory disturbances.
items.explanation: The use of zinc in treating olfactory disturbances is controversial and does not appear to have much benefit beyond replenishing established deficiencies.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.statement: Amitriptyline can be used to treat smell distortions but may also cause them.
items.explanation: Amitriptyline is used to treat dysosmias and smell distortions, particularly following head trauma, but it can also distort smell and taste function due to its anticholinergic effects.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.assertion: Dysfunctional olfactory senses can lead to decreased quality of life.
items.reason: Olfaction contributes significantly to the flavor and palatability of foods and beverages, affecting overall quality of life.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · Disorders of Smell and Taste

items.assertion: Each glomerulus in the olfactory bulb is a functional unit.
items.reason: Receptor cells that express the same type of receptor project to the same glomeruli.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are both true, with the reason correctly explaining why each glomerulus acts as a functional unit.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Damage to the thalamus does not affect olfactory perception.
items.reason: Olfactory deficits can occur with thalamic damage due to disrupted connections between the primary olfactory cortex and orbitofrontal cortex.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because thalamic damage can indeed lead to olfactory deficits, as explained by the reason.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: T1R3 receptors and gustducin are involved in the sensing and transport of dietary sugars.
items.reason: They play a role in regulating hormone release from gut enteroendocrine cells.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: The primary taste cortex is involved in establishing 'flavor'.
items.reason: It contains cells activated by several sensory modalities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: Olfactory impairment often precedes the clinical diagnosis of Parkinson's Disease.
items.reason: The olfactory bulbs may be among the first sites of neural damage in Parkinson's Disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why olfactory impairment often precedes the clinical diagnosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.assertion: Smell loss is a common initial symptom of Alzheimer's Disease.
items.reason: Patients with very mild 'presymptomatic' signs of AD have poorer smell function associated with higher levels of AD-related pathology.
items.explanation: While both the assertion and reason are true, smell loss is more marked in DLB than early AD; thus, it's not a common initial symptom.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.assertion: Intranasal zinc-containing nasal gels and sprays can lead to loss of smell function.
items.reason: These products are common over-the-counter prophylactics for upper respiratory viral infections, which are the most common cause of anosmia and hyposmia.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not directly explain why intranasal zinc-containing nasal gels and sprays can lead to loss of smell function.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE
items.assertion: Taste alterations are more common than olfactory changes due to drugs.
items.reason: More than 250 medications have been reported to alter the ability to taste.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · DISORDERS OF OLFACTION (part 2) | DISORDERS OF TASTE

items.assertion: Electrophysiologic testing provides additional diagnostic information for smell disorders.
items.reason: Electrophysiologic tests require complex stimulus presentation and recording equipment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; electrophysiologic testing rarely provides additional diagnostic information.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.assertion: Most commercially available taste tests require stimulus preparation.
items.reason: Taste tests use filter paper strips or similar materials impregnated with tastants, requiring no stimulus preparation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these tests do not require stimulus preparation.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · CLINICAL EVALUATION

items.assertion: Chlorhexidine mouthwash mitigates some salty or bitter dysgeusias.
items.reason: It conceivably works due to its strong positive charge.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: The use of flavor enhancers is always safe for patients with underlying hypertension or diabetes.
items.reason: Flavor enhancers like monosodium glutamate can make food more palatable.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because caution is advised to avoid overusing ingredients containing sodium or sugar.
items.reference: Disorders of Smell and Taste · TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)
